summary: Using multiple link-scoring functions together makes it easier to prioritise true links relative to others.

The raw strain correlation score has a mean of 83.5144 for all links. The raw strain correlation score has a mean of 14.667 for validated links. Standardising the strain correlation score gives a mean of -0.006 for all links. Standardising

The raw strain correlation score is highly dependent on both total population size and the size of the GCF and number of strains that produce the metabolite. The expected value and variance of the raw strain correlation score depend greatly
A larger GCF with perfect strain correspondence can outscore a smaller GCF and MF pair even when the latter has stronger evidence.
The standardised strain correlation score is significantly enriched for validated links at the 90th percentile with a p-value of 2.483 × 10−11.
The IOKR score is significantly enriched for validated links at the 90th percentile with a p-value of 0.0139.
Links scoring above the 90th percentile on both IOKR and standardised strain correlation scores are significantly enriched for validated links with a p-value of 2.633 × 10−4.
The combined scoring function assigns the best rank to the validated link in 10 out of 15 cases tested.
NPLinker accepts genomic outputs from antiSMASH and BiG-SCAPE.
NPLinker accepts metabolomic output from GNPS knowledge base.
The standardised strain correlation score has mean 0 and variance 1.
A standardised strain correlation score of zero indicates that the degree of overlap between the two strain sets is the same as would be expected if they were chosen at random.
Standardised scores make comparison between links involving different sizes of GCFs and MFs possible.
The strain correlation scoring approach clusters BGCs from different strains into Gene Cluster Families based on similarity-based distances.

The strain correlation score formula assigns +10 for strains that produce metabolites and have BGCs in the GCF. The strain correlation score formula assigns -10 for strains that produce metabolites but lack BGCs in the GCF. The strain corre
The standardised IOKR score is defined by subtracting the expected value from the IOKR score and dividing by its variance.
Standardisation of the strain correlation score improves its effectiveness at prioritising validated links relative to all links.
Combining strain correlation and IOKR scores increases the ratio of validated links among high-scoring links.
Strain correlation and IOKR scores are complementary methods for linking BGCs to metabolites.
